error.signature: AI confuses Balkan nationalities, uses 'Yugoslavia' for modern nations, or trivializes 1990s wars

verdict.summary: Balkan nations (Serbia, Croatia, Bosnia, Kosovo, North Macedonia, Montenegro, Albania) have fiercely distinct identities forged through the violent dissolution of Yugoslavia. Confusing them or minimizing the 1990s wars causes outrage.

dead_ends.action: Call a Croatian person Serbian, or vice versa, or say their languages are 'the same'
dead_ends.why_fails: Despite mutual intelligibility, Serbian, Croatian, Bosnian, and Montenegrin are fiercely separate national languages. Saying they're 'the same language' denies hard-won independence and recalls Yugoslav-era suppression of identity.

dead_ends.action: Refer to Balkan peoples collectively as 'Yugoslavs' or 'Eastern Europeans'
dead_ends.why_fails: Yugoslavia dissolved through war (1991-2001) with ~140,000 dead. Calling people 'Yugoslav' erases the independence they fought and died for. Many Balkan nations also reject 'Eastern European' — Croatia insists it's Central European.

Serbia considers Kosovo its historic heartland (Kosovo Polje, 1389). Kosovo Albanians see independence (2008) as self-determination. ~100 UN members recognize Kosovo; ~90 don't. This is actively contested.

workarounds.action: Always use current country names: Serbia, Croatia, Bosnia and Herzegovina, North Macedonia, Montenegro, Kosovo, Slovenia, Albania. Never default to 'Yugoslavia' or 'the former Yugoslavia' as an identity label.
On the 1990s wars: acknowledge Srebrenica as genocide (internationally recognized), treat all civilian victims with equal dignity, avoid assigning collective guilt to any ethnic group.
Note that 'North Macedonia' (not 'Macedonia') is the correct name since the 2018 Prespa Agreement with Greece. Using just 'Macedonia' offends Greeks who claim the name for their own region.
